lesson.subtitle: Aula 16 -- Project Updates -- Reporting Progress
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Natalie Viegas | Aula 16 -- Project Updates

listenings.text: Hi Natalie, this is Tom Bradley from the steering committee. I am calling about the cloud migration update before tomorrow's review with leadership. To date, the numbers look strong, and I want the headline to be clear. We have been working on phase two for three weeks now, and we have already completed the data security audit. The team has been testing the new environment since Monday, so we are building real momentum. There is one dependency I am worried about: the vendor sign-off. If it slips, we may need to escalate it to the sponsor. Could you bring the latest metrics? I would like to compare this month with last month and show that we are still ahead of schedule. Thanks, talk soon.

Hi, this is Natalie, recording a quick note after preparing the progress report. For three weeks, we have been running phase two of the cloud migration, and the metrics are encouraging. We have completed the data security audit, and we have been testing the new environment since Monday. Compared with last month, we are ahead of schedule, which is great news for leadership. There is one blocker I have been monitoring: the vendor sign-off is a dependency for the next milestone. I have decided to escalate it early and to mitigate the risk with a backup plan. I have learned that a good update is honest: it shows the momentum and the results, but it never hides a blocker. Reporting progress clearly is how you keep the trust of leadership.

extra_audio.text: First, you state the headline, so leadership knows the status in one sentence. Then you report the results you have completed, with clear metrics. After that, you explain what the team has been doing and the momentum so far. Next, you flag any blocker or dependency honestly, before it grows. Finally, you confirm the next steps and whether you are still ahead of schedule.
